mnemonics.title: Coefficient Meaning
mnemonics.content: COEFFICIENT: Count Of k non-attacking rooks - k-th coefficient tells you exactly how many ways to place k rooks that don't attack each other. Remember: 'k-th coefficient = k rooks count'.

mnemonics.title: Product Formula
mnemonics.content: DISJOINT MULTIPLY: When boards are disjoint (no shared rows or columns), multiply their rook polynomials. Think: 'Separate boards, separate calculations, then multiply'.

mnemonics.title: Maximum Rooks
mnemonics.content: MINIMUM MATTERS: Maximum non-attacking rooks = min(rows, columns). Like a team where the smaller group limits team size.

mnemonics.title: Constant Term
mnemonics.content: ALWAYS ONE: Constant term (x⁰) is always 1 - the one way to place zero rooks. No matter the board, 'doing nothing' has exactly one way.

acronyms.expansion: Rook polynomial of board B in variable x

acronyms.usage: r_k is the coefficient of x^k in the rook polynomial

acronyms.expansion: Rook polynomial of n×n complete board

acronyms.usage: Used to derive rook polynomial formula

visualTips.visualization: Picture a coordinate grid where each rook 'claims' its row and column - imagine drawing a cross through each rook, and no crosses should overlap.
Two boards side by side with no shared rows or columns appear as completely separate rectangles with a gap between them.
As k increases, the coefficients first grow then shrink (unimodal) - like a bell curve, reaching maximum around n/2.
When multiplying polynomials, think of choosing rooks from first board AND second board - combine all possibilities.

analogies.analogy: Like a menu that lists all possible meal combos - each x^k entry shows how many 'combo packs' of k items are available.
Like assigning people to committees where no two people can be on the same committee - each person needs their own unique committee.
Like counting ways to choose an outfit from two separate closets - multiply the options from each closet.
Like a bridge with limited lanes - the number of cars (rooks) that can cross without colliding is limited by the narrower side.
